story:
To be beautiful and extraordinary in love is a gilded lie. Most fail; those who succeed end up worse off. I chose a life that gave me joy, heartbreak, and scars. I didn’t understand, back when I was young—back before the golden walls of LePrince Arena were even built—that youth is another word for foolish. I have lived too long inside memories made half a century ago. I keep trying to go back. I never can.

The band blares. The background dancers grin in shimmering dresses that make them look like mermaids, teal kitten heels peeking beneath their skirts. The camera tilts up and then I see her. A green one-piece bathing suit covered in tiny sequin scales, flared slightly at the hips so the lights catch the gold accents. Platinum hair tucked under a jeweled cap, a few strands escaping. I don’t need the announcer. No one does. Even four decades on, everyone still knows. Annitta LePrince. The fountains burst on either side of the impossibly high board, she waves, and then she dives, a tumble so graceful it still steals my breath. That’s always when I turn off the TV. I can’t watch the glittering symphony that follows—the synchronized swimming that crowns her in the center. I was there for almost every one. I watch so I don’t forget. I won’t forget. Still, I dare not risk it.

There is one performance I will never watch. The tape sits in the back of a light blue cabinet. A label: July 24, 1955. Her hair is down that night, rare and radiant; her suit is cream white with pearls, a small gold-and-pearl crown at her temple. All her idea. The white, a secret nod to our engagement. At the end she climbs to the top of the stage and says, “Please give a round of applause to my exquisite fiancé, Finn Rivers,” then giggles and waves at me. The outrage was immediate. No one knew she was dating, let alone promised. No one expected that America’s Treasure could already belong to someone. I was stunned; I thought the white was odd. Later she threw her arms around me and told me she had planned it for weeks. I am a pebble—ordinary and fine with it. She was sea glass—scoured by the ocean into something rare. I never needed to be spectacular. I was loved by someone who was.

We married twenty-three days later in a small chapel in the New Hampshire woods. The best and worst days of my life sit seventy-two hours apart. If I could stop us there, I would. But August nineteenth arrives. She goes for a swim at dawn. I wake feverish and terrified, stagger to the beach, see her in the water, and my body gives out. Five hours later I open my eyes in a hospital. I ask for her. Whispered debates. Then: she was caught in a rip current trying to save me. The headlines call it cruel irony: Annitta LePrince, one of the strongest swimmers in the world, lost to a riptide. Sometimes I wish the roles were reversed. Sometimes grief lies and tells you it would be easier to be dead than to live without the dead. I know it isn’t true, but some days it is hard to be grateful for breath.

I donated her costumes so they could be enjoyed, not mourned. At LePrince Arena, divers still climb and swimmers still sweep the lanes she once ruled. Her museum glows with sequins and photographs. Outside, a gold fountain holds her forever in the moment before flight—leaned forward on her toes, arms swept back, about to take one last, perfect dive.
